strengths: Thoughtful CRAAP evaluation indicating critical engagement with sources
Clear assessment of Expertioâs credibility and relevance
Evidence of prototype testing through comparison of two formats
Use of digital tools for self-management showing organized workflows
Development of extensive research questions reflecting thorough inquiry
weaknesses: ATL skills are not explicitly linked to individual action steps
CRAAP analysis lacks concrete examples or screenshots to support judgments
Overreliance on digital reminders without diversified self-management strategies
Research questions are too numerous and unfocused; top priorities need selection
No evidence (e.g., screenshots) of applied storyboard learning

feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The student demonstrates engagement with information literacyâseen in the thoughtful CRAAP evaluation and clear review of Expertioâas well as an experimental approach to design through prototype testing. However, the application of ATL skills remains abstract without explicit connections to specific tasks. Introducing concrete evidence (screenshots, data points), diversifying organizational strategies, focusing on the most relevant research questions, and establishing clear criteria for prototype evaluation will strengthen the practical application of skills and improve the final productâs quality.

strengths: Comprehensive success-criteria table demonstrating thorough consideration of project requirements
Clear articulation of the learning goal, providing a solid foundation for the work
Reflective prior-knowledge section showing engagement with existing skills
Descriptive connection to the global context, indicating awareness of thematic relevance
Logical grouping of long-term tasks by month, showing some planning structure
weaknesses: Success criteria lack measurable targets and deadlines
Action plan needs finer sequencing with specific weekly or daily deliverables
Learning goal milestones are not quantifiable (dates or percentages)
Prior-knowledge reflections are not explicitly linked to product success criteria
Connection to global context is descriptive but not tied to concrete success criteria
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: While the submission displays strong initial planningâparticularly in outlining a detailed success-criteria table and clearly stating the learning goalâit falls short in defining measurable benchmarks and scheduling granular tasks. The studentâs reflective prior-knowledge section and descriptive global context connection show depth of thought, yet these elements must be tied more directly to specific criteria and deadlines. Providing quantifiable milestones, explicit links between prior knowledge and criteria, and breaking down tasks into weekly outcomes will elevate the plan from conceptual to actionable.

strengths: Insightful reflection on deeper knowledge of the global context
Visionary discussion of future learning pathways
Passionate concluding reflection showing personal investment
weaknesses: Reflections note weaknesses but omit targeted next steps for improvement
Evaluation of product against success criteria lacks specific examples or metrics
Initial learning goal and success criteria are not revisited to demonstrate full-circle growth
Future learning statements are broad without concrete action plans
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The learnerâs reflective writing reveals a genuine understanding of the global context and enthusiasm for ongoing growth. Nevertheless, the reflections remain largely descriptive rather than evidence-based. To complete the reflective cycle, the student should tie reflections back to the original success criteria with concrete data, outline clear next steps to address identified weaknesses, and specify actionable plans for future learning. This will transform insightful commentary into measurable growth.

annotations.comment: Your success-criteria table is thorough but lacks measurable targets and deadlines. Add quantifiable benchmarks (e.g. âuser trial with 5 peers completed by date Xâ) to each row.
Your long-term action plan groups steps by month but lacks detailed sequencing and specific deliverables for each date. Break tasks into weekly or daily actions with clear outcomes.
The connection to the global context is descriptive; strengthen it by linking specific aspects of âorientation in space and timeâ to your success criteria (e.g. mapping sections by age groups).
When describing ATL skills in your action plan, explicitly link each step to the skill youâre developing (e.g. âorganize meeting = self-management,â âcompare products = critical thinkingâ).

Your learning goal is clear but has no measurable success criteria. Consider adding specific milestones (e.g. âpublish first draft by date X,â âformat 50% of pagesâ) to track and demonstrate progress.
Your CRAAP evaluation is thoughtful but would benefit from concrete examples (e.g. include a screenshot) and a clearer assessment of the sourceâs limitations in multimedia support.
Your prior-knowledge section is reflective but doesnât link explicitly to your product success criteria. Show how existing knowledge informs specific criteria, such as digital formatting skills.
Your self-management relies heavily on digital reminders. Consider diversifying strategiesâsuch as physical checklists or peer accountabilityâto reduce dependence on a single tool.
Your research questions are extensive but unfocused. Prioritize the top 3 that directly support your learning goal to make your media-literacy research more efficient and relevant.
Your evaluation of expertio is clear but could explain how you verified its claims. Cite specific examples or data from expertio to illustrate credibility, accuracy and relevance.
You describe learning storyboard through tutorials but provide no evidence of application. Include an example or screenshot of a storyboard you created to demonstrate mastery.
Your reflection on organization skill describes tools, but doesnât evaluate effectiveness. Add data (e.g. number of tasks completed on time) to show how to-do lists improved your productivity.
When integrating prior and secondary research on the German system, explicitly compare findings to highlight new insights and resolve contradictions in your content.
Your media-literacy justification lists strengths/limitations but could relate these directly to your productâs designâidentify how each point shaped your ebook content or structure.
Your creative-thinking prototype step tested two formats but lacks defined criteria for comparison. Add specific evaluative criteria (readability, engagement) to make feedback more actionable.
Your weaknesses section notes issues but misses next steps. Add targeted strategies (e.g. âreduce pages to 18,â âinclude other culturesâ) to show how youâll improve.
Your reflection on project impact is descriptive but needs specific examples linked to success criteria (e.g. â60% of peers reported deeper understandingâ).
Your deeper knowledge of the global context is insightful; strengthen it by reflecting on how âorientation in space and timeâ guided your design decisions at each stage.
Your reflection on future learning is visionary but should specify concrete actions (e.g. âapply these skills in CAS by...â ) to demonstrate a clear path forward.
Your concluding reflection is passionate but could revisit your initial learning goal to demonstrate full-circle growth and highlight evidence of meeting each success criterion.
